title: Jude
author: Jude, brother of Jesus and of James (Jude 1; Matthew 13:55)
setting: Written to an unspecified community (possibly Jewish Christian) facing the infiltration of ungodly men who pervert God's grace into licentiousness and deny Jesus Christ.
purpose: To urge Christians to 'contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints' against antinomian false teachers — using OT examples (fallen angels, Sodom, Cain, Balaam, Korah) to demonstrate that judgment on such people is certain.
themes: The danger of antinomianism — grace as license for sin
Build yourself up in faith, prayer, and love
The doxology: God who keeps his people from stumbling

outline.label: Greeting and the Reason for Writing
OT Examples of Judgment on the Ungodly

before.label: James — Jude's own brother established the Jerusalem church
Antinomian teachers creep into the community
2 Peter — the same threat addressed from a similar angle

after.label: Revelation — contending for the faith amid imperial pressure
The faith once delivered must be guarded — contend and keep yourselves in God's love
The doxology of 24–25 — Christ able to keep us from stumbling

key_people.name: Jude, brother of Jesus
key_people.role: Identifies himself as 'brother of James' (v. 1) and therefore by implication brother of the Lord; writes with pastoral urgency about a specific threat of immoral teachers who have 'crept in unnoticed'

context: Jude was written c. AD 65-80 to a general Christian audience facing the same threat as 2 Peter (which uses much of Jude's material): antinomian teachers who are 'ungodly persons who pervert the grace of our God into sensuality and deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ' (v. 4). The letter is the most sustained single argument in the NT for the necessity of contending for the faith.
christ_connection: Jude's Christology is high and explicit: the Lord who saved Israel from Egypt is identified as Jesus (v. 5, in several key manuscripts), and the opponents who deny Christ (v. 4) are contrasted throughout with the community who is 'kept for Jesus Christ' (v. 1). The benediction (vv. 24-25) is the NT's most complete doxology: 'to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy' — the entire letter's concern for preservation against apostasy is resolved in the assurance of Christ's power to keep his own.

key_verses.note: 'Contend for the faith that was once for all delivered to the saints' — the letter's thesis: the faith is a fixed deposit requiring active defence, not accommodation to every new wind of teaching

'To him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy, to the only God, our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory' — the most complete doxology in the NT

themes_detail.title: Contending for the Faith Against Antinomian Teachers
themes_detail.text: Jude's urgent message is that the 'faith once for all delivered to the saints' (v. 3) is under threat from teachers who have 'crept in unnoticed' and are using God's grace as a license for immorality while denying Christ's lordship. These are not uninstructed outsiders but insiders at the fellowship meal (v. 12), which makes their threat more dangerous. Jude's response is not to tolerate diversity in the name of charity but to call the community to active defence of the apostolic faith. The three OT examples (Israel in the wilderness, the fallen angels, Sodom and Gomorrah) establish that God's judgment on immoral rebellion is certain and historical.

themes_detail.title: Historical Examples as Warnings and Encouragements
themes_detail.text: Jude is the NT's most concentrated use of typological examples as theological argument: the wilderness generation, Sodom and Gomorrah, Cain, Balaam, and Korah are all cited as historical patterns of the kind of rebellion the false teachers represent. This argument presupposes the reality and relevance of OT history: these events happened and were recorded precisely to warn subsequent generations (1 Cor 10:11). The use of non-canonical material (the archangel Michael's dispute about Moses's body from the Assumption of Moses, v. 9; Enoch's prophecy, vv. 14-15) shows Jude's confidence that truth may be found in various sources, though his appeal remains to known historical fact.

themes_detail.title: The Community's Defence Through Building Up and Prayer
themes_detail.text: After his extended polemic against the false teachers, Jude's positive instruction to the community (vv. 20-23) is surprisingly pastoral and differentiated: build yourselves up in the faith, pray in the Spirit, keep yourselves in God's love, and show mercy to those who doubt, to those being dragged toward the fire, and to those contaminated by it — with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh. The community's preservation is not merely doctrinal vigilance but a life of prayer, love, and graduated pastoral care toward those at various stages of compromise.

themes_detail.title: Doxology as the Letter's Resolution
themes_detail.text: Jude ends with one of the most magnificent doxologies in the NT (vv. 24-25), and the contrast with what preceded it is deliberate. The letter has catalogued every form of spiritual failure — denial, immorality, rebellion, apostasy. The answer is not improved human vigilance but the power of the one 'who is able to keep you from stumbling.' The certainty of final presentation 'blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy' is the pastoral anchor: the preservation of the saints is ultimately not their achievement but God's — and this certainty is the ground from which contending for the faith is possible and not merely anxious.
